answer:
Definition

Architecting Monolith Modularization for 1M+ users means restructuring a single deployable application into well-defined internal modules with clear boundaries, separate data access layers, and explicit inter-module APIs — preparing for eventual extraction into microservices without premature decomposition. This approach (modular monolith) gives the deployment simplicity of a monolith with the organizational clarity of services.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj's Fastify application started as a single src/ folder with routes, controllers, and Prisma models mixed together. At 1M users, the team needed to add features without merge conflicts and scale specific modules. Manoj would restructure into: src/modules/identity/, src/modules/leave/, src/modules/calendar/, src/modules/notification/ — each with its own routes, service layer, Prisma schema subset, and tests.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Module structure and boundaries
// src/modules/leave/index.ts — public API for Leave module
export { leaveRoutes } from './routes';
export type { LeaveRequest, LeaveStatus } from './types';
// Only exported interfaces can be used by other modules

// src/modules/leave/routes.ts
import { LeaveService } from './service';
import { CalendarModule } from '../calendar'; // Import through public API

export async function leaveRoutes(app: FastifyInstance) {
  const leaveService = new LeaveService();

  app.post('/api/leaves', async (req) => {
    const { startDate, endDate, type } = req.body;

    // Cross-module call through public interface
    const hasConflict = await CalendarModule.checkConflict({
      employeeId: req.user.id,
      startDate,
      endDate,
    });
    if (hasConflict) throw new Error('Date conflict detected');

    return leaveService.create({
      employeeId: req.user.id,
      startDate,
      endDate,
      type,
    });
  });
}

// src/modules/leave/service.ts
import { prisma } from '../../shared/prisma';

export class LeaveService {
  // Only accesses leave_ prefixed tables
  async create(data: CreateLeaveInput) {
    return prisma.leave_request.create({
      data: {
        employee_id: data.employeeId,
        start_date: data.startDate,
        end_date: data.endDate,
        type: data.type,
        status: 'PENDING',
      },
    });
  }

  // Anti-pattern: DO NOT directly query identity tables
  // BAD: prisma.identity_user.findUnique(...)
  // GOOD: IdentityModule.getUser(id)
}

// ESLint rule to enforce boundaries
// .eslintrc.js
module.exports = {
  rules: {
    'no-restricted-imports': ['error', {
      patterns: [
        { group: ['../identity/*', '!../identity/index'], message: 'Import through module public API' },
        { group: ['../leave/*', '!../leave/index'], message: 'Import through module public API' },
        { group: ['../calendar/*', '!../calendar/index'], message: 'Import through module public API' },
      ],
    }],
  },
};

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After modularizing the HR system, a developer on the notification team added a direct Prisma query to leave_request table to fetch leave details for email templates — bypassing the Leave module's public API.

Investigation: This went undetected for 2 months. When the Leave team renamed the leave_request.type column to leave_request.category, the notification module's direct query broke in production with a column-not-found error.

Fix: (1) Added ESLint no-restricted-imports rules to enforce module boundaries. (2) Added a pre-commit hook that checks for cross-module Prisma table access. (3) Created a LeaveModule.getLeaveDetails(id) public method that the notification module should call instead. (4) Added a CI check that fails if any module imports internal files from another module.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Developers bypass module boundaries via direct DB accessESLint rules + CI checks enforcing import boundaries
Shared database creates hidden couplingTable name prefixes per module; review migrations across modules
Module interfaces grow too largeKeep public API minimal; use events for loose coupling
Performance penalty from inter-module callsIn-process calls are nanoseconds — no HTTP overhead in modular monolith

Validation Strategy

  • Boundary check: CI step verifies no cross-module internal imports
  • Schema isolation: each module's migration only touches its prefixed tables
  • Extraction readiness: deploy notification module as separate service with no code changes to public API
  • Performance: benchmark inter-module calls, verify < 1ms overhead

Definition

Performance bottlenecks in Monolith Modularization arise when modularization introduces unnecessary abstraction layers, inter-module communication overhead, or when modules share contended resources (database connections, memory, CPU) within a single process. Key bottlenecks: excessive inter-module serialization, shared connection pool exhaustion, module initialization order issues, and memory contention between modules during garbage collection.

Concrete Use-Case

In the CRM Sales Dashboard, Manoj modularized the Express.js monolith into Pipeline, Lead, Analytics, and User modules. The Analytics module ran heavy MongoDB aggregation pipelines that consumed 80% of the Node.js heap, causing garbage collection pauses that affected the Pipeline module's response times — even though Pipeline was CPU-light.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Problem: Analytics aggregation blocks event loop
app.get('/api/analytics/pipeline-summary', async (req) => {
  // This aggregation processes 100K+ documents, takes 3-5 seconds
  // During this time, Pipeline routes are unresponsive
  const summary = await db.collection('deals').aggregate([
    { $match: { ownerId: req.user.id } },
    { $group: { _id: '$stage', total: { $sum: '$value' }, count: { $sum: 1 } } },
    { $sort: { total: -1 } },
  ]).toArray();
  return summary;
});

// Fix: Offload to worker thread
import { Worker, isMainThread, parentPort, workerData } from 'worker_threads';

// analytics-worker.js
if (!isMainThread) {
  const { MongoClient } = require('mongodb');
  const client = new MongoClient(workerData.mongoUrl);
  async function run() {
    await client.connect();
    const db = client.db('crm');
    const result = await db.collection('deals').aggregate([
      { $match: { ownerId: workerData.ownerId } },
      { $group: { _id: '$stage', total: { $sum: '$value' }, count: { $sum: 1 } } },
      { $sort: { total: -1 } },
    ]).toArray();
    parentPort.postMessage(result);
    await client.close();
  }
  run();
}

// Main thread route
app.get('/api/analytics/pipeline-summary', async (req) => {
  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    const worker = new Worker('./analytics-worker.js', {
      workerData: { mongoUrl: process.env.MONGO_URL, ownerId: req.user.id },
    });
    worker.on('message', resolve);
    worker.on('error', reject);
    setTimeout(() => { worker.terminate(); reject(new Error('Analytics timeout')); }, 10000);
  });
});

// Per-module connection pool
import { Pool } from 'pg';
const pools = {
  pipeline: new Pool({ connectionString: DB_URL, max: 6 }),
  analytics: new Pool({ connectionString: DB_URL, max: 8 }),
  lead: new Pool({ connectionString: DB_URL, max: 4 }),
  user: new Pool({ connectionString: DB_URL, max: 2 }),
};

Debugging Narrative

Situation: CRM users reported that deal creation (Pipeline module) sometimes took 5+ seconds during business hours, even though Pipeline queries only took 20ms. The issue was intermittent and only happened when multiple sales managers were viewing analytics reports.

Investigation: Manoj used clinic doctor and found GC pauses of 200-500ms when the Analytics module processed large aggregation results (1.2GB heap usage). The Node.js event loop was blocked during major GC cycles. Additionally, the shared PostgreSQL connection pool (20 connections) was monopolized by Analytics queries, leaving Pipeline waiting for connections.

Fix: (1) Moved Analytics aggregation to Worker threads — each heavy query runs in an isolated thread with its own heap. (2) Split the shared connection pool into per-module pools with reserved limits. (3) Added a Redis cache for frequently-accessed pipeline summaries (TTL 30s). (4) Set --max-old-space-size=2048 and enabled incremental GC. Result: Pipeline p99 from 5s → 80ms.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Heavy module's GC blocks all modulesWorker threads for CPU-heavy or memory-heavy operations
Shared connection pool contentionPer-module pool limits with reserved minimums
Module initialization order causes startup failuresExplicit dependency graph with async init sequence
Memory leak in one module crashes entire processPer-module memory budgets + heap monitoring alerts

Validation Strategy

  • Isolation test: run Analytics heavy aggregation while benchmarking Pipeline p99 — must stay < 100ms
  • Connection pool test: fill Analytics pool to capacity, verify Pipeline still gets connections
  • Memory test: simulate Analytics processing 500K documents, verify total heap stays under 2GB
  • GC test: monitor GC pause duration with --trace-gc, alert if any pause exceeds 100ms

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Observability examines the three pillars (metrics, logs, traces), how they correlate, instrumentation strategies (auto vs. manual), alert fatigue management, SLI/SLO-driven alerting, failure modes (observability pipeline saturation, cardinality explosion), and rollback plans when instrumentation itself causes performance problems.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj implemented observability across the Fastify API: structured JSON logs with correlation IDs, Prometheus metrics (request rate, error rate, latency histograms), and distributed traces across the leave approval saga (approval → balance → calendar → notification). When month-end spikes caused latency, the team could identify which service in the saga was the bottleneck within minutes.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Structured logging with correlation ID
import pino from 'pino';

const logger = pino({
  level: process.env.LOG_LEVEL || 'info',
  formatters: {
    level: (label) => ({ level: label }),
  },
  redact: ['req.headers.authorization', 'req.body.password'],
});

// Fastify request hook — inject correlation ID
app.addHook('onRequest', (req, reply, done) => {
  req.correlationId = req.headers['x-correlation-id'] || crypto.randomUUID();
  reply.header('x-correlation-id', req.correlationId);
  req.log = logger.child({ correlationId: req.correlationId, userId: req.user?.id });
  done();
});

// Prometheus metrics
import { register, Counter, Histogram } from 'prom-client';

const httpRequestsTotal = new Counter({
  name: 'http_requests_total',
  help: 'Total HTTP requests',
  labelNames: ['method', 'route', 'status_code'],
});

const httpRequestDuration = new Histogram({
  name: 'http_request_duration_seconds',
  help: 'HTTP request duration',
  labelNames: ['method', 'route'],
  buckets: [0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5],
});

app.addHook('onResponse', (req, reply, done) => {
  const route = req.routeOptions?.url || 'unknown';
  httpRequestsTotal.inc({ method: req.method, route, status_code: reply.statusCode });
  httpRequestDuration.observe(
    { method: req.method, route },
    reply.elapsedTime / 1000,
  );
  done();
});

// SLO-based alerting rule (Prometheus)
// Alert if error rate > 1% of requests over 5 minutes
// sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[5m])) /
// sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m])) > 0.01

// Metrics endpoint
app.get('/metrics', async () => register.metrics());

Debugging Narrative

Situation: The HR system's Prometheus instance crashed with OOM after a deployment. Grafana dashboards went blank, and the team was blind during a latency incident.

Investigation: Manoj checked Prometheus storage metrics before the crash. The http_requests_total counter had a userId label — with 50K active users, this created 50K × 12 routes × 5 status codes = 3M time series. Prometheus memory grew to 8GB and was killed by the OOM killer.

Fix: (1) Removed userId from metric labels — user-level tracking belongs in logs and traces, not metrics. (2) Reduced route label cardinality by grouping parameterized routes (/api/leaves/:id instead of /api/leaves/abc123). (3) Added a prom-client label validation hook that rejects labels with >100 unique values. Time series dropped from 3M to 360. (4) Set up Prometheus recording rules to pre-compute SLO burn rates.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Cardinality explosion in metric labelsNever use user ID, request ID, or unbounded values as labels
Log volume overwhelms pipelineStructured logs + log level runtime control + sampling for debug
Trace overhead degrades application performanceHead-based sampling (10% in production); always trace errors
Alert fatigue from too many low-signal alertsSLO-based alerting: alert on error budget burn rate, not individual errors

Validation Strategy

  • Cardinality audit: script that queries Prometheus TSDB status, alerts if any metric has >1000 series
  • Correlation test: trigger an error, verify the same correlation ID appears in log, trace, and error metric
  • Performance test: enable full tracing, verify < 5% latency overhead vs. no tracing
  • Rollback test: set sampling rate to 0.0, verify zero trace export overhead

Definition

Architecting Observability for 1M+ users requires a scalable telemetry pipeline that can handle millions of metrics data points, terabytes of logs, and millions of trace spans per day — without the observability infrastructure itself becoming a bottleneck. Key considerations: sampling strategies, data retention policies, cost management, and real-time anomaly detection.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled ContactsHub serving 1M users, Manoj would design the observability stack: Pino structured logs → Fluentd → Loki (7-day retention), Prometheus metrics with recording rules for pre-aggregation, OpenTelemetry traces with head-based sampling (1% normal, 100% errors). At 10K req/s, this means 600K log entries/minute, 500 metric series, and 100 trace spans/second.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// OpenTelemetry setup with adaptive sampling
import { NodeSDK } from '@opentelemetry/sdk-node';
import { TraceIdRatioBasedSampler, ParentBasedSampler } from '@opentelemetry/sdk-trace-base';
import { OTLPTraceExporter } from '@opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-http';

// Adaptive sampling: 1% normal, 100% errors
class AdaptiveSampler {
  private baseSampler = new TraceIdRatioBasedSampler(0.01);

  shouldSample(context, traceId, name, kind, attributes) {
    // Always sample errors and slow requests
    if (attributes?.['http.status_code'] >= 500) return { decision: 1 };
    if (attributes?.['http.duration_ms'] > 1000) return { decision: 1 };
    return this.baseSampler.shouldSample(context, traceId, name, kind, attributes);
  }
}

const sdk = new NodeSDK({
  traceExporter: new OTLPTraceExporter({ url: `${JAEGER_URL}/v1/traces` }),
  sampler: new ParentBasedSampler({ root: new AdaptiveSampler() }),
});
sdk.start();

// SLO-based alerting configuration
const SLO_CONFIG = {
  availability: { target: 0.999, window: '30d' },
  latency: { target: 0.95, threshold_ms: 500, window: '30d' },
};

// Prometheus recording rules for SLO burn rate
// rules.yml:
// - record: slo:error_rate:5m
//   expr: sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[5m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m]))
// - alert: HighErrorBurnRate
//   expr: slo:error_rate:5m > (1 - 0.999) * 14.4
//   for: 2m
//   labels: { severity: critical }
//   annotations: { summary: "Error budget burning 14.4x faster than allowed" }

// Runtime log level control
app.post('/admin/log-level', async (req) => {
  const { level } = req.body; // 'debug' | 'info' | 'warn' | 'error'
  logger.level = level;
  return { message: `Log level set to ${level}`, effectiveImmediately: true };
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: ContactsHub's observability pipeline itself became the bottleneck. Fluentd was consuming 2GB RAM buffering logs, and the Loki ingestion rate hit its limit, causing log loss during peak hours. The team couldn't debug a production issue because the relevant logs were dropped.

Investigation: Manoj checked Fluentd metrics — buffer queue depth was at 100K (max 50K), causing drops. The root cause: a developer had set log level to debug in production for troubleshooting and forgot to revert. Debug-level logs were 20× the volume of info-level.

Fix: (1) Created an admin API endpoint to change log level at runtime without redeployment. (2) Set a Fluentd filter to drop debug/trace level logs in production regardless of app setting. (3) Added structured log sampling for high-volume endpoints (only log 1 in 10 successful health check requests). (4) Added Loki ingestion rate monitoring with auto-alert when approaching 80% capacity.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Observability pipeline becomes its own bottleneckBackpressure handling in Fluentd; drop low-priority logs under load
Debug logs left on in productionRuntime log level control + auto-revert to info after 1 hour
Trace storage costs grow linearly with trafficAdaptive sampling: 1% normal, 100% errors/slow requests
Alert fatigue leads to ignoring real issuesSLO-based alerting on error budget burn rate, not individual errors

Validation Strategy

  • Pipeline capacity test: generate 10× normal log volume, verify no log loss (check Fluentd drop counter)
  • Sampling accuracy: verify error traces always captured even at 1% base sampling rate
  • Alert quality: review alert history — no alert should fire more than once per week unless actioned
  • Cost audit: monthly observability cost review — target < 5% of total infrastructure cost

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into SLO/SLA examines Service Level Objectives (internal targets), Service Level Agreements (contractual commitments), and Service Level Indicators (metrics that measure them). Key areas: SLI selection (availability, latency, throughput), error budget policies, burn rate alerting, SLA negotiation trade-offs, failure modes (SLO gaming, metric manipulation), and rollback plans when SLO enforcement blocks feature velocity.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj defined SLOs: (1) Availability: 99.9% uptime (43.8 min downtime/month), (2) Latency: 95% of requests < 500ms, 99% < 2s, (3) Data correctness: leave balance accuracy 100% (zero tolerance). The error budget (0.1% = 43.8 min/month) determined deployment policy: if error budget is < 20% remaining, freeze all non-critical deployments until the budget refreshes.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// SLI measurement and error budget tracking
const SLO = {
  availability: 0.999,  // 99.9%
  latencyP95Ms: 500,    // 500ms p95
  windowDays: 30,
};

// Track SLIs in real-time
const sliCounters = {
  totalRequests: 0,
  successfulRequests: 0,
  latencyBuckets: new Array(10).fill(0),
};

app.addHook('onResponse', (req, reply, done) => {
  sliCounters.totalRequests++;
  if (reply.statusCode < 500) sliCounters.successfulRequests++;

  // Track for p95 calculation
  const ms = reply.elapsedTime;
  const bucket = Math.min(Math.floor(ms / 100), 9);
  sliCounters.latencyBuckets[bucket]++;
  done();
});

// Error budget API
app.get('/admin/error-budget', async () => {
  const availability = sliCounters.successfulRequests / sliCounters.totalRequests;
  const errorBudgetTotal = (1 - SLO.availability) * sliCounters.totalRequests;
  const errorsUsed = sliCounters.totalRequests - sliCounters.successfulRequests;
  const budgetRemaining = Math.max(0, errorBudgetTotal - errorsUsed);
  const budgetPercentRemaining = (budgetRemaining / errorBudgetTotal) * 100;

  return {
    sli: { availability: (availability * 100).toFixed(3) + '%' },
    slo: { availability: (SLO.availability * 100).toFixed(1) + '%' },
    errorBudget: {
      total: Math.floor(errorBudgetTotal),
      used: errorsUsed,
      remaining: Math.floor(budgetRemaining),
      percentRemaining: budgetPercentRemaining.toFixed(1) + '%',
      deployPolicy: budgetPercentRemaining > 50 ? 'NORMAL' :
                    budgetPercentRemaining > 20 ? 'CAREFUL' : 'FROZEN',
    },
  };
});

// Burn rate alert (Prometheus query)
// Fast burn: 14.4× budget consumption in 1h → page on-call
// Slow burn: 3× budget consumption in 6h → ticket for team
// sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[1h])) /
//   sum(rate(http_requests_total[1h])) > 14.4 * (1 - 0.999)

Debugging Narrative

Situation: The HR system's availability SLI showed 99.95% for the month — well above the 99.9% SLO. But users complained about frequent errors during business hours (9am-6pm). Off-hours availability was 100%, masking the real user experience.

Investigation: Manoj realized the SLI was calculated over all 24 hours. During business hours (40% of the day, 90% of traffic), availability was actually 99.8% — below the SLO. The nightly batch jobs and health checks had perfect availability, inflating the overall number.

Fix: (1) Weighted the availability SLI by request volume per hour (traffic-weighted availability). (2) Added a separate SLI for business-hours availability. (3) Created a Grafana dashboard showing SLI broken down by hour, highlighting business-hours dips. (4) Added burn rate alerting that uses a 1-hour window to catch business-hours degradation before it's masked by overnight recovery.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
SLI averaged over 24h hides business-hours issuesTraffic-weighted SLI + separate business-hours SLO
Error budget freeze blocks all developmentAllow critical security fixes during freeze; only block feature work
SLA set too tight, causing constant breach riskSLA should be looser than SLO (99.5% SLA for 99.9% SLO)
Synthetic probes inflate availability numbersMeasure SLIs from real user traffic (RUM), not synthetic

Validation Strategy

  • SLI accuracy: compare server-side SLI with client-side RUM availability — should be within 0.1%
  • Error budget: simulate a 30-minute outage, verify error budget dashboard reflects correct remaining budget
  • Burn rate alert: inject 5xx errors at 14.4× budget rate, verify alert fires within 5 minutes
  • Policy enforcement: with < 20% budget remaining, verify CI/CD blocks non-critical deployments

Definition

Architecting SLO/SLA for 1M+ users requires defining meaningful Service Level Indicators that reflect real user experience, setting achievable objectives that balance reliability with development velocity, implementing automated error budget tracking that enforces deployment policies, and designing multi-region availability strategies that meet contractual SLA commitments.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled CRM Sales Dashboard for 1M sales reps, Manoj would define tiered SLOs: (1) Pipeline API: 99.95% availability, p99 < 1s (revenue-critical), (2) Analytics API: 99.5% availability, p99 < 5s (latency-tolerant), (3) Report exports: 99% availability, completion within 30 minutes (batch-tolerant). The SLA to enterprise customers: 99.9% monthly availability with service credits for breaches.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Multi-tier SLO monitoring system
interface SLODefinition {
  name: string;
  tier: 1 | 2 | 3;
  availability: number;
  latencyP99Ms: number;
  windowDays: number;
}

const SLO_DEFINITIONS: SLODefinition[] = [
  { name: 'pipeline-api', tier: 1, availability: 0.9995, latencyP99Ms: 1000, windowDays: 30 },
  { name: 'analytics-api', tier: 2, availability: 0.995, latencyP99Ms: 5000, windowDays: 30 },
  { name: 'report-exports', tier: 3, availability: 0.99, latencyP99Ms: 30 * 60 * 1000, windowDays: 30 },
];

// Prometheus recording rules for each tier
// For each SLO, compute burn rate at different windows
function generatePrometheusRules(slo: SLODefinition) {
  const errorRate = 1 - slo.availability;
  return {
    // Fast burn: 14.4× in 1h (pages immediately)
    fastBurn: `sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="${slo.name}",status_code=~"5.."}[1h])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="${slo.name}"}[1h])) > ${errorRate * 14.4}`,
    // Slow burn: 3× in 6h (creates ticket)
    slowBurn: `sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="${slo.name}",status_code=~"5.."}[6h])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="${slo.name}"}[6h])) > ${errorRate * 3}`,
  };
}

// Error budget enforcement in CI/CD
app.post('/api/deploy/gate', async (req) => {
  const { service, deployType } = req.body;
  const slo = SLO_DEFINITIONS.find(s => s.name === service);
  if (!slo) return { allowed: true };

  const budget = await getErrorBudgetRemaining(service);

  if (budget.percentRemaining < 25 && deployType !== 'critical-fix') {
    return {
      allowed: false,
      reason: `Error budget at ${budget.percentRemaining.toFixed(1)}% — only critical fixes allowed`,
      tier: slo.tier,
    };
  }

  if (budget.percentRemaining < 75 && deployType === 'experiment') {
    return {
      allowed: false,
      reason: `Error budget at ${budget.percentRemaining.toFixed(1)}% — experiments paused`,
    };
  }

  return { allowed: true, budgetRemaining: budget.percentRemaining };
});

// SLA breach detection and credit calculation
async function calculateSLACredits(month: string) {
  const availability = await getMonthlyAvailability(month);
  if (availability >= 0.999) return { creditPercent: 0 };
  if (availability >= 0.990) return { creditPercent: 10, reason: '99.0-99.9% availability' };
  return { creditPercent: 30, reason: 'Below 99.0% availability' };
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: An enterprise CRM customer claimed SLA breach (availability below 99.9%) and demanded service credits. The internal monitoring showed 99.92% — above the SLA. The customer's monitoring showed 99.7%.

Investigation: Manoj compared the measurement methodologies. Internal monitoring measured at the load balancer — including successful health checks and internal API calls. The customer measured from their network — which included DNS resolution failures, TLS handshake timeouts, and client-side connection errors that the load balancer never saw.

Fix: (1) Switched to measuring availability from external synthetic probes (multiple geographic locations), matching the customer's perspective. (2) Added a shared SLA dashboard that both parties could reference. (3) Revised the SLA to define availability as "successful responses to external probes from 3 regions, excluding planned maintenance windows." (4) Implemented RUM (Real User Monitoring) as the definitive SLI source.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Internal monitoring disagrees with customer's measurementUse external probes (synthetic monitoring from multiple regions)
One SLO for all services → over-engineering low-criticalityTiered SLOs: different reliability targets per service importance
Error budget freeze kills all momentumDefine deployment tiers: always allow critical security fixes
SLA breach calculation disputesShared dashboard with agreed-upon measurement methodology

Validation Strategy

  • SLI source agreement: verify external probe availability matches internal within 0.05%
  • Error budget automation: deploy non-critical change with < 25% budget → verify CI/CD rejects it
  • SLA credit calculation: simulate 99.5% month, verify credit calculation returns 10%
  • Tiered alerting: inject errors in Tier 1 service → verify page within 2 minutes; Tier 3 → ticket only
